subtopic_detail: Repair location for full thickness rectal mucosa injury after vaginal birth
stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to a 28-year-old primipara who has just had an instrumental vacuum birth of a 3.9 kg infant. Inspection of the perineum after delivery reveals torn external and internal anal sphincters with a 1 cm full thickness defect into the rectal mucosa. The episiotomy was mediolateral. Estimated blood loss is 350 mL. She has had spinal anaesthesia for the birth that is still effective.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 86, RR 16, BP 118/72, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Mediolateral episiotomy with extension through both anal sphincters and into rectal mucosa; perineum well perfused
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L; coagulation within normal limits; group and hold valid
data_table.Setting: Birth suite room with spinal still effective; theatre available within 30 minutes

lead_in: Where is the most appropriate location to perform the repair?

options.text: Transfer to operating theatre for repair under regional or general anaesthesia with adequate lighting
options.rationale: Third or fourth degree tears require repair in theatre with adequate lighting, anaesthesia, and surgical instruments to optimise outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS)

options.text: Repair in the birth suite room with current spinal anaesthesia using bedside instruments now
options.rationale: Birth suite repair of fourth degree tears is associated with poorer visualisation and higher long-term continence morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS)

options.text: Defer repair for 24 hours to allow oedema reduction and plan a planned-list theatre slot
options.rationale: Delayed repair increases infection and dehiscence risk; repair should be undertaken at the earliest safe opportunity. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS)

options.text: Pack the perineum with antiseptic gauze and arrange colorectal surgeon review at next ward round
options.rationale: Packing is not the management of a fresh fourth degree tear; obstetric repair in theatre is the standard. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS)

options.text: Repair only the rectal mucosa with interrupted sutures now and the sphincters in clinic later
options.rationale: Sphincter repair must be done at the same time as mucosal repair to maximise functional recovery. (Source: RANZCOG: OASIS)

explanation.summary: Obstetric anal sphincter injury that extends into rectal mucosa is a fourth degree tear and must be repaired in theatre with adequate lighting, regional or general anaesthesia, appropriate instruments, and an experienced operator. The internal anal sphincter is repaired end to end and the external sphincter by end to end or overlap technique. Postoperative care includes laxatives, single dose intravenous antibiotic prophylaxis, and pelvic floor physiotherapy referral.
key_points: Theatre repair for third and fourth degree tears
Repair at earliest safe opportunity
IAS end to end; EAS end to end or overlap
Postop laxatives, antibiotics, and physiotherapy
explanation.pearls: Single dose intravenous cephalosporin and metronidazole at OASIS repair reduces wound infection from about 24 to 8 percent at six weeks, which is why prophylaxis is part of the RANZCOG bundle.

subtopic_detail: First-line agent for anovulatory PCOS in a woman pursuing pregnancy with elevated BMI
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 29-year-old woman with established polycystic ovary syndrome who wants to conceive. Her cycles are 60 to 90 days with anovulation on luteal progesterone testing. BMI is 31. She has no pelvic pain or partner fertility concerns and her partner has a normal semen analysis. She has been trying for nine months and has not taken any prior fertility medication. There is no diabetes, no liver disease, and no contraindication to ovulation induction.

data_table.Investigations: Normal pelvic ultrasound polycystic appearance bilaterally; HbA1c 41 mmol/mol; TSH 2.1; prolactin 240 mIU/L; AMH 65 pmol/L
data_table.Examination: Mild acne and hirsutism; abdominal exam normal
data_table.Partner: Normal semen analysis; couple in a stable relationship

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line ovulation induction agent?

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily on cycle days 2 to 6 with cycle tracking and follicle monitoring
options.rationale: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS with higher live birth rates than clomiphene, especially in women with obesity. (Source: 2023 International PCOS Guideline; RANZCOG)

options.text: Metformin 1500 mg daily as ovulation induction monotherapy with cycle tracking and TFT review
options.rationale: Metformin is an adjunct that may improve ovulation but is inferior to letrozole for live birth in anovulatory PCOS. (Source: 2023 International PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Clomiphene 50 mg daily on cycle days 2 to 6 with progressive titration each cycle if anovulatory
options.rationale: Clomiphene is second-line because letrozole achieves higher live birth rates in anovulatory PCOS, particularly with obesity. (Source: 2023 International PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Gonadotrophin injections from cycle day 2 with daily ultrasound follicle and oestradiol tracking
options.rationale: Gonadotrophins are reserved for letrozole and clomiphene failure due to higher cost, monitoring burden, and OHSS risk. (Source: 2023 International PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling as the primary fertility intervention before any pharmacological trial
options.rationale: Surgical drilling is reserved for letrozole or clomiphene failure due to surgical and adhesion risks. (Source: 2023 International PCOS Guideline)

explanation.summary: The 2023 International PCOS Evidence-Based Guideline recommends letrozole as first-line ovulation induction in anovulatory PCOS, with higher live birth rates than clomiphene in most subgroups and particularly in women with elevated BMI. Metformin is an adjunct in women with insulin resistance or impaired glucose tolerance. Gonadotrophins, IVF, and ovarian drilling are reserved for first-line failure. Pre-conception counselling includes weight optimisation, folate, and rubella status.
key_points: Letrozole first-line for anovulatory PCOS
Higher live birth rate than clomiphene in obesity
Metformin is adjunctive, not monotherapy
Gonadotrophins and drilling are second-line
explanation.pearls: Letrozole achieves live birth rates around 27 percent versus 19 percent for clomiphene in obese anovulatory PCOS within six cycles, which underpins the 2023 PCOS guideline switch.

subtopic_detail: Recurrent symptomatic UTI in postmenopausal woman with vaginal atrophy on examination
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 68-year-old woman who has had four culture-proven E. coli urinary tract infections in the past 12 months. She has post-coital dysuria and external dryness. She has no haematuria between infections, no flank pain, and no incontinence. Bladder scan post-void shows 25 mL residual. Examination shows a pale, smooth vaginal mucosa with loss of rugae and friability on speculum. She has had no breast cancer.

data_table.Examination: Atrophic vaginal mucosa, no prolapse, no pelvic mass, no suprapubic tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Mid-stream urine sterile today; renal tract ultrasound normal; cystoscopy report normal
data_table.Past history: Menopause at 52; no breast or endometrial cancer; not on systemic HRT

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Commence daily oral trimethoprim 150 mg as long-term low-dose prophylaxis for six months
options.rationale: Antibiotic prophylaxis does not address atrophy and increases resistance; non-antibiotic options come first. (Source: RACGP: Recurrent UTI)

options.text: Commence daily cranberry capsule 500 mg supplement and review symptoms after three months
options.rationale: Cranberry has inconsistent evidence and does not treat the underlying genitourinary atrophy contributing to recurrence. (Source: Cochrane; RACGP)

options.text: Commence systemic oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily for symptom control
options.rationale: Systemic HRT is not indicated for isolated genitourinary symptoms and carries broader hormonal risks. (Source: AMS; RANZCOG)

options.text: Commence vaginal oestriol 0.5 mg pessary nightly for two weeks then twice weekly thereafter
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen treats atrophy and reduces recurrent UTI rates in postmenopausal women with safety profile preferable to antibiotics. (Source: AMS; RANZCOG)

options.text: Refer for cystoscopy with bladder biopsy to exclude an underlying interstitial cystitis condition
options.rationale: Cystoscopy has already been performed and is normal; further endoscopic workup is not indicated. (Source: RACGP: Recurrent UTI)

explanation.summary: Recurrent urinary tract infection in postmenopausal women is frequently driven by genitourinary syndrome of menopause, which alters vaginal flora and local mucosal defences. Vaginal oestrogen pessary or cream is first-line and reduces recurrence with minimal systemic absorption. Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis is reserved for vaginal oestrogen failure due to resistance concerns. Cranberry has inconsistent evidence. Cystoscopy is indicated for haematuria or persistent symptoms despite treatment.
key_points: Vaginal oestrogen is first-line for recurrent UTI in postmenopausal women
Reduces recurrence with minimal systemic absorption
Antibiotic prophylaxis reserved for vaginal oestrogen failure
Cranberry has inconsistent evidence
explanation.pearls: Vaginal oestriol pessary reduces postmenopausal recurrent UTI rates by approximately 50 percent within six months and is safe in most women with prior breast cancer if symptoms are severe after specialist discussion.

subtopic_detail: Sudden maternal collapse with hypotension and DIC during second stage of labour
stem: You are the obstetric registrar in birth suite when a 36-year-old multipara at term in second stage suddenly becomes breathless, profoundly hypotensive, and then loses consciousness within two minutes of pushing. Saturation drops to 78 percent on air. Fresh PV bleeding ensues with non-clotting blood from her IV cannula site. Fetus is well grown with reactive CTG immediately prior. There is no history of placental abnormality, no anaphylaxis trigger, and no pulmonary embolism risk factors.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 138, RR 36, BP 70/40, SpO2 78 percent on air, GCS 8
data_table.Examination: Generalised oozing from cannula and PV; cervix fully dilated; vertex at +1; no localised wheeze; no rash
data_table.Investigations: Arterial blood gas pH 7.18 lactate 8; INR 2.4; fibrinogen 1.0 g/L; platelets 70
data_table.Setting: Birth suite; theatre and anaesthetics 2 minutes away; obstetric MET activated

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg into the lateral thigh as anaphylaxis treatment first line
options.rationale: There is no rash, wheeze, or trigger; anaphylaxis treatment alone misses the haematological collapse of AFE. (Source: ASCIA; RANZCOG)

options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed to immediate operative vaginal birth
options.rationale: AFE causes cardiogenic shock and DIC; massive transfusion plus expedited delivery and supportive care is the standard approach. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse)

options.text: Start intravenous heparin infusion for presumed pulmonary embolism with anticoagulation as priority
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is contraindicated in a patient already coagulopathic with active haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse)

options.text: Administer intramuscular oxytocin 10 units and observe for spontaneous third stage progression next
options.rationale: Standard third stage management does not address the acute coagulopathic shock and delays definitive resuscitation. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse)

options.text: Administer high dose intravenous hydrocortisone 1 g and tranexamic acid 1 g as initial monotherapy
options.rationale: Single drug therapy alone misses the volume, blood product, and surgical needs of AFE; it is not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse)

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but life-threatening obstetric emergency presenting in late labour or shortly after birth with sudden hypoxia, hypotension, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Management is supportive: airway and breathing support, massive transfusion protocol activation, expedited delivery, and ICU level care. There is no specific antidote. Survival has improved markedly with early MTP activation and aggressive resuscitation. Differential diagnosis includes anaphylaxis, pulmonary embolism, and septic shock, but the coagulopathy with peripartum onset is characteristic.
key_points: AFE: sudden hypoxia, hypotension, and DIC peripartum
Activate MTP early
Expedite delivery if not yet born
ICU level supportive care; no specific antidote
explanation.pearls: Amniotic fluid embolism mortality has fallen from over 80 percent historically to around 20 to 30 percent with rapid MTP activation; the diagnostic triad of hypoxia, hypotension, and consumption coagulopathy in labour is the operational definition.

subtopic_detail: Choosing between varenicline and combination NRT for adult smoker seeking quit attempt
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 52-year-old man who smokes 25 cigarettes daily and wants to quit before an elective knee replacement in eight weeks. He has had three prior failed quit attempts using nicotine patches alone. He has stable mood with no history of suicidal ideation, no seizures, no untreated cardiovascular disease, and no contraindication to varenicline. He prefers oral medication and is willing to set a quit date in two weeks.

data_table.Mental state: Euthymic, no suicidal ideation, no recent depressive episode
data_table.Past history: Hypertension on amlodipine; no seizure disorder; no liver disease; eGFR over 60 mL/min/1.73m2
data_table.Smoking: 25 per day for 30 years; Fagerstrom score 7

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily titrated up to 1 mg twice daily starting one week before quit date
options.rationale: Varenicline has the highest single-agent cessation efficacy and suits a planned quit attempt with no contraindications. (Source: RACGP: Smoking cessation; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat nicotine patch monotherapy for another six week course as the proven single agent strategy
options.rationale: Repeat patch monotherapy after prior failure has low success; escalation to varenicline or combination NRT is recommended. (Source: RACGP: Smoking cessation)

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg daily for three days then 150 mg twice daily for nine weeks from the quit date
options.rationale: Bupropion is third-line behind varenicline and combination NRT and is contraindicated in seizure disorder, but here varenicline is preferred. (Source: RACGP: Smoking cessation)

options.text: E-cigarette as the primary cessation strategy given preference for oral nicotine substitution device
options.rationale: E-cigarettes are not a first-line cessation strategy in Australia and are recommended only after registered therapies fail. (Source: RACGP: Smoking cessation)

options.text: Brief advice and motivational counselling only without any pharmacotherapy this consultation
options.rationale: Behavioural advice alone is insufficient in a heavy smoker with prior failed attempts; pharmacotherapy adds substantial benefit. (Source: RACGP: Smoking cessation)

explanation.summary: Varenicline and combination NRT (patch plus short acting form) are the most effective pharmacotherapies for smoking cessation in adults. Varenicline is started one week before the quit date and continued for 12 weeks, with consideration of a 12-week maintenance course in high relapse risk. Combination NRT is a reasonable alternative for those who cannot take varenicline. Bupropion is third-line and contraindicated in seizure disorder. Behavioural support roughly doubles success rates of pharmacotherapy.
key_points: Varenicline and combination NRT are first-line
Start varenicline one week before the quit date
Treat for at least 12 weeks; consider maintenance
Add behavioural support to pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: Varenicline achieves 6-month cessation around 25 percent versus 18 percent for single patch alone in heavy smokers; the FDA boxed warning for psychiatric effects was removed in 2016 after the EAGLES trial showed no excess risk.

subtopic_detail: Choice of SSRI when starting antidepressant treatment in a breastfeeding mother
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 32-year-old woman at 10 weeks postpartum with moderate depressive symptoms confirmed by EPDS of 18 and clinical interview. She is exclusively breastfeeding her well 10-week-old son. She has not had antidepressant treatment previously. There is no suicidal ideation, no psychotic features, and no manic history. She wishes to start an antidepressant and continue breastfeeding.

data_table.Mental state: Tearful, low mood for six weeks, sleep disturbance beyond infant feeds, anhedonia, no suicidal ideation, no psychotic features
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication; no thyroid disease; TSH 1.8 mIU/L
data_table.Infant: Term, well, gaining weight on the 25th centile

lead_in: Which antidepressant has the most supportive safety data in this setting?

options.text: Fluoxetine 20 mg daily orally with infant monitoring during initiation and dose titration
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has the longest half-life of the SSRIs and accumulates in breastfed infants more than alternatives. (Source: COPE; LactMed)

options.text: Citalopram 20 mg daily orally with weekly infant clinical review during the first two weeks
options.rationale: Citalopram is acceptable but has slightly higher infant exposure than sertraline and is not preferred first-line in lactation. (Source: COPE; LactMed)

options.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg nightly orally with infant weight and sedation review during the first two weeks
options.rationale: Mirtazapine has less safety data in lactation and is not the preferred first-line agent for postnatal depression. (Source: COPE)

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg daily orally with infant clinical review for sedation and feeding during initiation
options.rationale: Sertraline has the lowest measured infant serum concentrations among SSRIs and is the preferred first-line agent in breastfeeding women. (Source: COPE; LactMed)

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg daily orally with blood pressure and infant review during the first two weeks
options.rationale: Venlafaxine has higher discontinuation symptoms and is not first-line in postnatal lactation. (Source: COPE)

explanation.summary: Sertraline is the preferred first-line antidepressant in breastfeeding women because of the lowest measured infant serum concentrations among SSRIs. Paroxetine is an alternative. Fluoxetine has the longest half-life and active metabolite, leading to greater infant accumulation. Citalopram and escitalopram are acceptable alternatives if previously effective. Treatment should be combined with structured psychological therapy and a planned review at one and four weeks. Mothers should be reassured that breastfeeding does not need to be stopped.
key_points: Sertraline is the first-line SSRI in lactation
Fluoxetine accumulates more in breastfed infants
Add psychological therapy
Review at one and four weeks for response and tolerability
explanation.pearls: Infant serum sertraline levels are typically less than 2 percent of weight-adjusted maternal dose, which is the lowest of the SSRIs and underpins the COPE recommendation as first-line in lactation.

subtopic_detail: Initial inpatient feeding plan for severe anorexia nervosa with refeeding risk markers
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 19-year-old woman with anorexia nervosa to an adolescent ward. She has BMI 13.6, has eaten less than 500 kcal daily for four weeks, and has lost 12 kg in three months. Bloods show phosphate 0.65 mmol/L, magnesium 0.7 mmol/L, and potassium 3.2 mmol/L. She is normotensive supine but with HR 38 supine. She has no severe organ failure and is consenting to treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: T 35.8, HR 38 supine, RR 14, BP 92/56 supine, BMI 13.6
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, peripheral oedema +, lanugo, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Na 138, K 3.2, Mg 0.7, PO4 0.65, glucose 3.6 mmol/L; LFTs ALT 84; ECG QTc 470 ms
data_table.Mental state: Engaged, cognitively intact, no suicidal ideation, agrees to treatment

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial feeding plan?

options.text: Commence oral feeds at 5 kcal/kg/day with thiamine and replace electrolytes before any oral intake
options.rationale: Starting feeds at 5 kcal/kg/day is too restrictive and delays nutritional recovery; current ANZAED guidance starts higher with vigilance. (Source: ANZAED: Refeeding)

options.text: Commence oral feeds at 20 kcal/kg/day with thiamine 300 mg IV and daily phosphate magnesium potassium
options.rationale: Starting feeds at 20 kcal/kg/day with thiamine and daily electrolyte monitoring is current ANZAED guidance for high-risk refeeding. (Source: ANZAED: Refeeding)

options.text: Commence oral feeds at 40 kcal/kg/day with weekly bloods and no parenteral thiamine prescribed
options.rationale: 40 kcal/kg/day at BMI 13.6 with electrolyte derangement carries high refeeding risk; weekly bloods is insufficient. (Source: ANZAED: Refeeding)

options.text: Commence total parenteral nutrition through central venous access with daily electrolyte review
options.rationale: TPN is reserved for failed enteral feeding or contraindications; oral or NG feeding is preferred. (Source: ANZAED: Refeeding)

options.text: Commence nasogastric continuous feeds at 60 mL/hour without electrolyte preloading or thiamine
options.rationale: High continuous feed without electrolyte and thiamine preloading risks refeeding syndrome and cardiac complications. (Source: ANZAED: Refeeding)

explanation.summary: Severe anorexia with electrolyte derangement, bradycardia, and prolonged starvation is high risk for refeeding syndrome. ANZAED guidance starts feeds at 20 kcal/kg/day with thiamine supplementation and daily monitoring of phosphate, magnesium, potassium, and glucose for the first week, with electrolyte replacement as needed. Bradycardia and orthostasis are markers of medical instability requiring inpatient monitoring. Psychological therapy and family work continue alongside medical stabilisation.
key_points: Start 20 kcal/kg/day, increase as electrolytes allow
Thiamine 300 mg IV before feeds in high-risk patients
Daily phosphate, magnesium, potassium, and glucose
Bradycardia and orthostasis are medical instability markers
explanation.pearls: Refeeding hypophosphataemia typically develops 24 to 72 hours after starting feeds; the threshold for replacement is phosphate under 0.6 mmol/L with IV potassium phosphate at about 0.3 mmol/kg over 6 hours.

subtopic_detail: Initial mental health assessment of an Aboriginal man presenting with low mood in regional GP
stem: You are the rural GP reviewing a 45-year-old Aboriginal man presenting with three months of low mood, broken sleep, and reduced engagement with family. He attends with his sister who provides collateral. He recently lost his mother and has begun attending Sorry Business in his community. He has no suicidal ideation, no psychotic features, and no alcohol or drug use. He is reluctant to see a mainstream mental health service.

data_table.Mental state: Withdrawn, congruent affect, no suicidal ideation, no perceptual abnormality
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication; chronic lower back pain; no prior mental health treatment
data_table.Social: Lives with extended family; cultural and community connections important

lead_in: What is the most culturally appropriate first step?

options.text: Refer urgently to a mainstream community psychiatric team for inpatient assessment as a priority
options.rationale: Mainstream urgent referral is disproportionate without risk features and may worsen engagement with care. (Source: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health)
source_refs: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health

options.text: Start oral sertraline 50 mg daily as monotherapy without further mental health team involvement now
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy alone does not address social and emotional wellbeing or cultural context; partnership care is preferred. (Source: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health)
source_refs: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health

options.text: Document the consultation and review in six weeks without offering any therapeutic plan today
options.rationale: Six-week passive review without active engagement risks deterioration; supportive care should commence now. (Source: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health)
source_refs: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health

options.text: Discharge with online self-help resources and a fact sheet about depression in adult patients now
options.rationale: Self-help leaflets alone do not address SEWB context or family-centred preferences and underuse local services. (Source: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health)
source_refs: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health

options.text: Offer co-ordinated care with the local Aboriginal community-controlled health service and SEWB worker
options.rationale: Engagement with the Aboriginal community-controlled health service and SEWB worker centres cultural context, family, and continuity of care. (Source: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health)
source_refs: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health

explanation.summary: Social and emotional wellbeing is the holistic framework for mental health in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, recognising connection to country, family, culture, spirituality, and community as integral. The first step in an engaged primary care assessment is partnership with an Aboriginal community-controlled health service and SEWB worker where available. Pharmacotherapy and psychological therapy can be added once a therapeutic relationship and cultural support are in place. Grief and loss responses, including Sorry Business, are part of cultural context and shape presentation.
key_points: SEWB framework rather than medical-model only
Partner with ACCHS and SEWB worker
Add pharmacotherapy and therapy once engaged
Recognise grief and loss in cultural context
explanation.pearls: The Kessler-5 is the validated screening tool for psychological distress in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations and replaces the K10 in this context; scores 12 or above warrant further SEWB assessment.

sources.label: RACGP: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander health

subtopic_detail: Pre-treatment cardiovascular screening before adult stimulant initiation in newly diagnosed ADHD
stem: You are the psychiatrist starting methylphenidate for a 34-year-old man with newly diagnosed adult ADHD after a comprehensive assessment. He has no syncope, no chest pain, and no exertional symptoms. His father had a sudden cardiac death at 42 years from a documented arrhythmia. The patient has not had cardiology assessment previously. He is otherwise well, not on any other medication, and has no substance use.

data_table.Examination: Cardiovascular examination unremarkable, no murmurs, no displaced apex
data_table.Investigations: ECG not yet performed; baseline bloods unremarkable
data_table.Family history: Father sudden cardiac death at 42 from documented arrhythmia

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before initiating stimulant?

options.text: Refer to cardiology for ECG and echocardiogram review before commencing any stimulant medication
options.rationale: First-degree family history of sudden cardiac death warrants cardiology workup before stimulant initiation. (Source: RANZCP: Adult ADHD)

options.text: Commence methylphenidate 10 mg twice daily and review symptoms at four weeks with a BP recheck
options.rationale: Stimulants can unmask arrhythmia or structural disease; family history of SCD requires workup first. (Source: RANZCP: Adult ADHD)

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily as non-stimulant alternative without cardiology referral now
options.rationale: Atomoxetine also affects blood pressure and pulse and is not exempt from cardiac workup with this family history. (Source: RANZCP: Adult ADHD)

options.text: Order outpatient 24-hour Holter monitor alone without further cardiology input before treatment now
options.rationale: Holter alone does not screen for structural disease; cardiology assessment with ECG and echocardiogram is preferred. (Source: RANZCP: Adult ADHD)

options.text: Defer all pharmacotherapy indefinitely and refer for ongoing CBT alone without further investigation
options.rationale: CBT alone is insufficient for most adult ADHD; pharmacotherapy is the first-line intervention after cardiac workup. (Source: RANZCP: Adult ADHD)

explanation.summary: Stimulants raise heart rate and blood pressure modestly and can unmask underlying structural cardiac disease or arrhythmia. Pre-treatment screening involves cardiovascular history, family history of sudden cardiac death under 40 years, examination, blood pressure and pulse, and ECG if any red flags. Cardiology referral with echocardiogram is indicated for first-degree family history of sudden cardiac death, syncope, or examination abnormality. Atomoxetine and guanfacine also affect cardiovascular parameters and are not exempt.
key_points: Family history SCD under 40 in first-degree relative: cardiology review
Syncope and structural exam findings need workup
Both stimulants and non-stimulants affect cardiovascular parameters
Continue BP and pulse monitoring throughout treatment
explanation.pearls: Methylphenidate typically raises resting heart rate by 5 to 10 bpm and systolic blood pressure by 2 to 5 mmHg; absolute cardiovascular event rates are low in healthy adults but family history of SCD under 40 mandates pre-treatment workup.

sources.label: Australian Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline for ADHD

subtopic_detail: Recommended initial screening tool for refugee mental health in primary care first visit
stem: You are the refugee health GP reviewing a 28-year-old man from Syria three weeks after arrival on a humanitarian visa. He reports broken sleep, nightmares, irritability, and avoidance of crowds. He spent three years in transit including time in detention. He speaks limited English and an Arabic interpreter is present. He has no acute suicidal ideation and is engaged in the consultation but cautious.

data_table.Examination: Wary, intermittent eye contact, no current acute distress
data_table.Past history: Reported torture survivor; no diagnosed mental illness; nil regular medication
data_table.Social: Recent arrival; living with extended family; engaged with settlement service

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial mental health screening approach?

options.text: Use a translated PHQ-9 self-report tool today and book a six-week follow-up for routine review
options.rationale: Generic PHQ-9 has limited validity in refugee populations and a six-week passive review delays specialist input. (Source: FASSTT; RACGP)

options.text: Refer for inpatient psychiatric admission for diagnostic clarification and trauma therapy initiation
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is disproportionate in a man without acute risk and may retraumatise a detention survivor. (Source: FASSTT)

options.text: Start oral sertraline 50 mg daily empirically for presumed PTSD before any formal mental health assessment
options.rationale: Empiric pharmacotherapy without trauma-informed assessment misses culture and engagement priorities. (Source: FASSTT)

options.text: Decline to discuss trauma and provide general lifestyle advice with social settlement support only today
options.rationale: Avoiding mental health topics misses an opportunity to engage and signpost specialist trauma services. (Source: FASSTT)

options.text: Use a culturally validated screening tool such as the RHS-15 with an accredited interpreter and onward referral
options.rationale: The Refugee Health Screener RHS-15 with an accredited interpreter is the recommended initial screening and gates onward STARTTS referral. (Source: FASSTT; RACGP)

explanation.summary: Refugee health assessment requires trauma-informed care, accredited interpreters, and validated screening tools. The Refugee Health Screener RHS-15 is the recommended initial screening tool for distress, anxiety, depression, and PTSD symptoms in primary care. Onward referral to specialist services such as the state Survivors of Torture and Trauma services (STARTTS, FASST, ASeTTS) is indicated for positive screens or complex presentations. Pharmacotherapy is added after engagement with specialist trauma services where possible.
key_points: Use RHS-15 with accredited interpreter as initial screen
Refer positive screens to specialist torture and trauma services
Engage trauma-informed care principles
Pharmacotherapy follows specialist input where possible
explanation.pearls: The RHS-15 takes about 10 to 15 minutes, is available in over 15 languages, and uses a positive screen cut-off of 12 or above which flags about 80 percent of refugees with significant mental health needs.
